coredata.dc:title: Persistent luminescence nanoparticles/hierarchical porous ZIF-8 nanohybrids for autoluminescence-free detection of dopamine
coredata.dc:creator: Yao, Tianlin
Dong, Gaoqiu
Qian, Shiyu
Cui, Yu
Chen, Xiang
Tan, Tingting
Li, Lingling
coredata.dc:description: Accurate quantitative detection of biomarkers in complex samples is of remarkable significance for clinical diagnosis and follow-up therapy, which remains challenging due to the severe interference from sample matrix. Herein, a background-free sensing platform toward ultrasensitive dopamine (DA) detection is developed based on a multifunctional nanohybrid by integrating ultrasmall Cr3+-doped zinc gallate (ZnGa2O4:Cr3+, ZGC) persistent luminescence nanoparticles (PLNPs) with hierarchical porous zeolite imidazole framework-8 (HZIF-8). The ZGC PLNPs decorated on the surface of HZIF-8 have long-lasting near-infrared emission and act as signal element, whereas the hierarchically porous HZIF-8 scaffold can serve as an analyte concentrator to enrich DA around ZGC PLNPs. Furthermore, the oxidative polymerization of enriched DA is favored in the alkaline microenvironment provided by 2-methylimidazole in HZIF-8, producing polydopamine (PDA) which finally quenches the luminescence of ZGC/HZIF-8 via photoinduced electron transfer (PET) mechanism. Under the optimized conditions, the proposed nanoplatform exhibits excellent sensitivity and selectivity for the determination of DA in the range of 0.0025–75 μM with a limit of detection down to 0.0010 μM. Benefiting from the superior excitation-free persistent luminescence, this platform also shows satisfactory feasibility in quantitative determination of DA in human serum samples. This new strategy can contribute to the development of PLNPs-based biosensors for practical application in clinical analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Persistent luminescence
Metal-organic frameworks
Hierarchical porosity
Dopamine
Biosensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Quantitative DA determination in human serum

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22074066 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphological and optical characteristics of ZGC PLNPs synthesized at pH 9.5. (a) TEM image, (b) size distribution, (c) photoluminescence (λex = 254 nm) and excitation spectra (λem = 700 nm), inset: images of ZGC PLNPs aqueous dispersion under sunlight and 254 nm UV irradiation, and (d) persistent luminescence decay curve of ZGC PLNPs.

Physicochemical properties of HZIF-8 and ZGC/HZIF-8. (a) TEM image of HZIF-8. (b) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm and pore size distribution of HZIF-8. (c) TEM image of ZGC/HZIF-8. (d) FT-IR spectra of ZGC, HZIF-8, and ZGC/HZIF-8. (e) Photoluminescence spectra of ZGC and ZGC/HZIF-8. (f) Persistent luminescence decay curve of ZGC/HZIF-8, inset: persistent luminescence decay images of the ZGC/HZIF-8 using an IVIS Lumina XR imaging system.
(a) Quenching responses of ZGC and ZGC/HZIF-8 aqueous dispersions towards DA. (b) Photoluminescence (blue) and persistent luminescence spectra (red) of human serum containing ZGC/HZIF-8.

Persistent luminescence quenching performance of ZGC/HZIF-8 towards DA. (a) Persistent luminescence spectra of ZGC/HZIF-8 in the presence of DA with concentrations between 0 and 75 μM, inset: the corresponding pictures of ZGC/HZIF-8 dispersions with different concentrations of DA under sunlight and 254 nm UV irradiation. (b) Linear fitting curves of the relative PL intensity of ZGC/HZIF-8 versus the concentrations of DA in the range of 0.0025–25 μM and 25–75 μM. (c) Persistent luminescence decay images of ZGC/HZIF-8 in the absence and presence of different concentrations of DA.
(a) Selectivity of ZGC/HZIF-8 based sensing platform. (b) Interference experiments. The concentration of DA and interfering substances are 10 μM and 50 μM in Tris-HCl buffer (2.5 mM, pH 7.4).
Schematic illustration of the preparation of ZGC/HZIF-8 based DA sensing platform.

head.ce:title: Persistent luminescence nanoparticles/hierarchical porous ZIF-8 nanohybrids for autoluminescence-free detection of dopamine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutics, School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing 211166, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Pharmaceutics, School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutics, School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing 211166, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Laboratory Medicine, Nanjing Drum Tower Hospital, Nanjing University Medical School, Nanjing 210008, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Laboratory Medicine, Nanjing Drum Tower Hospital, Nanjing University Medical School

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Laboratory Medicine, Nanjing Drum Tower Hospital, Nanjing University Medical School, Nanjing 210008, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Accurate quantitative detection of biomarkers in complex samples is of remarkable significance for clinical diagnosis and follow-up therapy, which remains challenging due to the severe interference from sample matrix. Herein, a background-free sensing platform toward ultrasensitive dopamine (DA) detection is developed based on a multifunctional nanohybrid by integrating ultrasmall Cr3+-doped zinc gallate (ZnGa2O4:Cr3+, ZGC) persistent luminescence nanoparticles (PLNPs) with hierarchical porous zeolite imidazole framework-8 (HZIF-8). The ZGC PLNPs decorated on the surface of HZIF-8 have long-lasting near-infrared emission and act as signal element, whereas the hierarchically porous HZIF-8 scaffold can serve as an analyte concentrator to enrich DA around ZGC PLNPs. Furthermore, the oxidative polymerization of enriched DA is favored in the alkaline microenvironment provided by 2-methylimidazole in HZIF-8, producing polydopamine (PDA) which finally quenches the luminescence of ZGC/HZIF-8 via photoinduced electron transfer (PET) mechanism. Under the optimized conditions, the proposed nanoplatform exhibits excellent sensitivity and selectivity for the determination of DA in the range of 0.0025–75 μM with a limit of detection down to 0.0010 μM. Benefiting from the superior excitation-free persistent luminescence, this platform also shows satisfactory feasibility in quantitative determination of DA in human serum samples. This new strategy can contribute to the development of PLNPs-based biosensors for practical application in clinical analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: As the most abundant catecholamine neurotransmitter in the brain, dopamine (DA) has received great attention for its influence on a variety of physiological functions of the central nervous system [1,2]. The dysregulation of dopamine system is related to the occurrence of Parkinson's disease [3,4], schizophrenia [5,6] and other diseases. Therefore, DA level has been used as an indicative marker for these neurological diseases, and high sensitivity and selectivity for DA detection are necessary for the clinical diagnosis and subsequent treatment.

Till now, numerous efforts have been committed to detecting DA including fluorescence analysis, electrochemical method, high performance liquid chromatography and so forth [7–9]. Especially, fluorescence analysis, owing to its advantages of high sensitivity, good selectivity, simplicity, rapidity and stability [10,11], has received increasing attention and been widely applied for DA detection mainly via either photoinduced electron transfer (PET) or Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism based on the fact that DA oxidation products feature excellent fluorescence quenching ability toward universal fluorophores [12,13]. Nevertheless, as for the detection of DA in biological matrices, fluorescence analysis inevitably suffers from interference of spontaneous fluorescence or light scattering due to the presence of impurities including proteins and other biological chromophores, thereby compromising the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and detection sensitivity [14]. In this regard, it is imminent to develop alternative autoluminescence-free optical sensing strategies for DA in biological samples. To this end, persistent luminescence nanoparticles (PLNPs) have been introduced to alleviate this problem. Persistent luminescence, a kind of phenomenon where luminescence still exists for seconds, hours or even days after excitation stops [15–17], has attracted extensive interest in the past few years. Given that the short-lived spontaneous fluorescence of biological tissues decays rapidly after removal of in-situ excitation, the long-lasting afterglow nature of PLNPs enables them to be considered as promising luminescence probes capable of effectively eliminating the autofluorescence interference [18–21], therefore significantly improving the sensitivity and SNR in biosensing [22,23]. Particularly, the past few years have witnessed the rapid development of a large amount of long afterglow nanomaterials [24], and the most typical representative are Cr3+-doped zinc gallate (ZnGa2O4:Cr3+, ZGC) PLNPs with near-infrared (NIR, 650–750 nm) persistent luminescence emission, which have been adopted as excitation-free optical nanoprobes in multifarious sensors for the sensitive determination of a wide variety of targets [25]. Besides, to our knowledge, studies on ZGC PLNPs for DA detection have not been reported so far.

On the other hand, the optical nanoprobes are usually integrated with other functional nanomaterials as carriers to further boost the sensing performance. Specifically, the ideal nanocarriers are expected to have either superb loading capacity for the optical nanoprobes or specific interaction with luminescence elements or target analytes. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), a crystalline porous material with a periodic network structure formed by self-assembly of transition metal ions and organic ligands [26], have made great achievements in sensing [27], catalysis [28] and gas storage [29] because of their advantages of high porosity, low density, large specific surface area, regular pores, adjustable pore size, diversity of topological structure and tailorability [30]. The highly porous MOFs have been applied as unique host matrices for various functional nanomaterials, such as fluorescent nanoclusters [31], upconversion nanoparticles [32] , metal nanoparticles [33], and PLNPs as well [34], to surprisingly bring out new functionalities in various applications. More importantly, MOFs possess unique superiorities while applied in sensing platform [35,36]: firstly, the considerable pores within MOFs exhibit superior molecular adsorption capability and can serve as natural habitat for target molecules, that is to say, MOFs can preconcentrate target analytes within the porous matrix to improve the overall sensitivity; secondly, the unique microenvironment within the framework, such as Lewis acidic or basic sites in the ligands and open metal sites, may give rise to specific interaction between MOFs and the analytes, thereby resulting in greatly improved detectable changes related to analyte concentration. Consequently, it is reasonably assumed that the PLNPs/MOFs composite may blaze a new trail to design multifunctional integrated platforms for ultrasensitive DA sensing. Nevertheless, the pores of the most widely reported MOFs belong to micropore regime (< 2 nm) [37], inevitably hampering the mass transfer rate and accessibility of open channels and/or active sites for PLNPs and DA. Therefore, mesoporous MOFs can probably replace the microporous counterparts as a promising building block to fabricate functional composites with PLNPs and achieve higher sensitivity for DA detection.

In this work, taking advantage of the excitation-free persistent luminescence of ZGC and the high loading capacity and target preconcentration effect of hierarchical porous zeolite imidazole framework-8 (HZIF-8), we propose a simple and sensitive signal quenching strategy to detect DA by using ZGC/HZIF-8 nanohybrids. The mechanism of the platform is described in Scheme 1, ultrasmall ZGC is adsorbed on the surface of HZIF-8 through electrostatic effect, wherein the abundant hierarchical pores within HZIF-8 matrix can provide considerable landing sites for guest ZGC and greatly improve the stability of ZGC/HZIF-8 composites. Meanwhile, the high molecular adsorption capability of HZIF-8 allows efficient accumulation of DA in the framework. Due to the alkaline environment provided by 2-methylimidazole (2-MI) in HZIF-8 skeleton, DA is oxidatively polymerized into polydopamine (PDA) covering the surface of the composites rather than in the solution, and PDA gradually competes for Zn2+ sites with 2-MI, resulting in the cracking of HZIF-8 structure. Simultaneously, photo-excited electrons are transferred from ZGC to oxidized PDA, leading to emission quenching of ZGC/HZIF-8 composites. Furthermore, the biosensor has been used to measure DA concentration in the human serum samples without in situ excitation, effectively eliminating the autofluorescence interference.

Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O, 2-methylimidazole (2-MI), and Poly (diallyl dimethyl-ammonium, chloride) solution (PDDA, 20 wt%, Mw 100,000–200,000) were obtained from Aladdin. Gallium nitrate hydrate, Chromium(III) nitrate nonahydrate were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Zn(NO3)2.6 H2O and hydrochloric acid were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. LTD. N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and ammonium hydroxide were from Adamas. Citric acid (CA) and 2-Propanol were from GENERAL-REAGENT. All the chemicals were used as they received. Ultrapure water was used in all steps.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were performed on JEOL JEM-1400 Flash. Zeta potential data were monitored with BeNano 90 Zeta Potential analyzer (Dandong Baxter Co. LTD). Persistent luminescence decay curves were carried out on BPCL-GP21Q (Guangzhou Micro Light Technology Co., LTD). The persistent luminescence images were recorded by IVIS Lumina XR imaging system (PerkinElmer, American). All fluorescence and persistent luminescence experiments were recorded by an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi Co., Tokyo, Japan). UV–visible absorption spectra were obtained with a UV-2450 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Corporation, Japan). The Fourier transform Infrared spectra (FT-IR) were measured with IS-20 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific) using KBr pellets method. Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) test was determined through Micromeritics-ASAP2460. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded using an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku Corporation, Japan–SmartLab, 9 kW).

ZGC PLNPs were synthesized by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method without sintering [38]. Typically, 2 mmol of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, 2 mmol of Ga(NO3)3, 0.004 mmol Cr(NO3)3 were mixed as vigorously stirred. The total volume was adjusted to 15 mL by adding ultrapure water. Then, ammonium hydroxide (28%) solution was added rapidly to adjust pH to 9–10 and white precipitate was immediately observed. After stirring for half an hour, the mixture was transmitted into a Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL) and sealed. The mixture was reacted at 220 ℃ for ten hours. After cooling to room temperature, the white precipitate was collected by centrifugation and washed with 0.01 M HCl. This step can remove possible zinc oxide impurities. Next, ZGC was mixed with 2-Propanol and obtained after centrifugation. Finally, ZGC was dispersed in ultrapure water for storage and the concentration of ZGC was approximately 4 mg/mL.

HZIF-8 was prepared according to previous experiments with minor modifications [39]. 8.21 g of 2-MI and 2.20 g of Zn(CH3COO)2·2H2O were dissolved in 50 mL and 30 mL of deionized water, respectively. Furthermore, 2-MI solution was added with 2.0 mL of 20 wt% aqueous solution of PDDA. Next, above two solutions were mixed and stirred vigorously for 2 h at room temperature. Finally, the white powder of generated HZIF-8 was washed three times with ultrapure water and dried at 60 ℃.

50 mg of obtained ZGC was introduced to 25 mL of DMF containing 1.25 g of CA followed by vigorous stirring for 4 h. The CA-functionalized ZGC PLNPs were collected by centrifugation and washed with DMF. Then the products were redispersed in ultrapure water and 100 mg HZIF-8 powder was further added with stirring for 2 h. The resulting composites were washed with ultrapure water.

The white powder (1 mg) of ZGC/HZIF-8 composites was first dispersed in Tris-HCl buffer solution (0.5 mL, 2.5 mM, pH 7.4). Diverse amounts of DA were added, and the resulting volume was adjusted to 1 mL with final DA concentrations ranging from 0.0025 to 75 μM. After the mixture was incubated at 37 ℃ for 4 h, persistent luminescence spectra were recorded without in situ excitation.

In order to assess the specificity of ZGC/HZIF-8 to DA, several amino acids and neurotransmitters in the body were selected to be added in Tris-HCl buffer solution of ZGC/HZIF-8 following above experimental operations.

To evaluate the feasibility of ZGC/HZIF-8 composites for detecting DA in practical samples, human serum collected from healthy volunteers was firstly diluted 100 times using Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4, 2.5 mM). Then, standard DA solution with different concentrations was added to the diluted serum to prepare the spiked samples. Subsequently, above samples were mixed with ZGC/HZIF-8. After incubation at 37 ℃ for 4 h, persistent luminescence intensities were monitored at 700 nm for quantitative analysis.

As shown in Scheme 1, the ZGC/HZIF-8 nanohybrids were elaborately designed to realize a high-performance DA sensing platform. Initially, we attempted to prepare ZGC PLNPs with sub-10 nm size and excellent optical properties. The ultrasmall size of ZGC not only can promote their enrichment on the surface of the host matrix, but also is conducive to enlarging their contact area with DA and ensuring all emitting center ions distributing within the effective interaction distance of DA. The size and crystal morphology of the as-prepared ZGC PLNPs were firstly characterized with transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Under the optimal pH of 9.5 (Fig. S1 and Fig. S2 ), ZGC PLNPs are well-dispersed, and display narrow size distribution with an average size of 6 ± 4 nm ( Fig. 1 (a,b)). Further, the photoluminescence excitation and emission spectra of ZGC PLNPs are given in Fig. 1 c, showing an intensive NIR emission around 700 nm under 254 nm excitation, attributing to the 2E → 4A2 spin-forbidden energy transition of doped Cr3+ ion [40]. The inset illustration are digital pictures of ZGC under sunlight and 254 nm UV irradiation, which confirms the strong fluorescence intensity of ZGC PLNPs. Moreover, the NIR persistent luminescence decay life of ZGC luminescence was recorded after 10 min of irradiation with a portable 254 nm lamp. As displayed in Fig. 1 d, obviously, ZGC PLNPs exhibit distinct persistent luminescence with decay life longer than 400 s, indicating they can be applied in biosensing without in-situ excitation, so as to avoid the interference of biological self-fluorescence.

HZIF-8 with mesopores were prepared utilizing PDDA as a structure-directing agent, which were then utilized as carrier matrix to carry loads of ZGC PLNPs. TEM image in Fig. 2 a shows monodispersed and bouquet-like HZIF-8 with roughly stacked surface. The XRD pattern of HZIF-8 (Fig. S3) displays characteristic peaks well-matched to those of pure ZIF-8 crystals, confirming its sodalite zeolite-type crystal structure. The N2 sorption isotherm measurement was further carried out to analyze the pore characters of HZIF-8. As displayed in Fig. 2 b, HZIF-8 features a combination of type I and type IV adsorption-desorption isotherm, indicative of its hierarchical micro- and mesoporous structure [41,42] . The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area is calculated to be 788 m2 g-1, comparable with previously reported hierarchical porous MOFs [43]. The presence of the mesoporous characters was further verified by the pore size distribution based on the Barret–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) model, showing a dominant peak at mesoporous regime (3.6 nm). Taken together, all these results indicate that HZIF-8 with micro- and mesoporous structure has been successfully prepared.

Since PDDA has been embedded in the final products, HZIF-8 exhibits higher positive charge than bare ZIF-8 (Fig. S4). To construct ZGC/HZIF-8 nanohybrids, the positively charged ZGC PLNPs are further modified with CA, leading to negatively charged surface due to the presence of -COOH groups (Fig. S5). Subsequently, ZGC/HZIF-8 composites were synthesized through electrostatic adsorption between electronegative ZGC PLNPs and electropositive HZIF-8. In brief, ultrasmall ZGC PLNPs with large specific surface area and HZIF-8 with rich hierarchical porous structure attract each other, contributing to the enrichment of ZGC PLNPs on the surface of HZIF-8. TEM images in Fig. 2 c shows that a large number of ZGC PLNPs are adsorbed on the surface of HZIF-8, confirming the successful synthesis of ZGC/HZIF-8. The formation of ZGC/HZIF-8 is also demonstrated by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra. As shown in Fig. 2 d, characteristic absorption peaks of Zn-N and C-N at 422 and 1100–1400 cm-1 belonging to HZIF-8 are clearly presented in the spectrum of ZGC/HZIF-8 [39]. Moreover, the absorption peak at ~ 585 cm-1 and 450 cm-1 representing the characteristic of Zn-O and Ga-O bending vibration (only existing in ZGC) respectively are also found in the ZGC/HZIF-8 sample [38]. Besides, the XRD pattern of ZGC/HZIF-8 (Fig. S3) includes two sets of diffraction peaks assigned to ZnGa2O4 cubic spinel crystals and ZIF-8 frameworks, suggesting the successful preparation of ZGC/HZIF-8 nanohybrids.

Furthermore, the optical properties of the as-obtained ZGC/HZIF-8 nanohybrids are investigated. The photoluminescence spectrum of ZGC/ZHIF-8 (Fig. 2 e) exhibits a strong emission peak at 700 nm under 254 nm UV excitation, consistent with that of ZGC. At the same time, ZGC/HZIF-8 composites inherit the long afterglow decay life of ZGC. As observed in Fig. 2 f, the composites show distinct long-lasting luminescence with a decay time longer than 300 s. The results can also be confirmed by an IVIS Lumina XR imaging system, which can reduce the interference from surrounding environment and greatly improve imaging sensitivity. It is clearly seen that the luminescence of ZGC/HZIF-8 can last more than 15 min after illumination by a 254 nm UV lamp for 10 min. Altogether, the intense and persistent luminescence of ZGC/HZIF-8 composites make them promising probes for autoluminescence-free biosensing applications.

It is generally known that DA and its oxidative products have been widely used as luminescence quenchers via either PET or FRET mechanism. On account of the superior long-lived luminescence of ZGC and the unique hierarchical porosity of HZIF-8, we envisioned that using ZGC/HZIF-8 nanohybrids as sensing probes could bring out synergistically improved performance for DA detection. To this end, the quenching responses of ZGC and ZGC/HZIF-8 towards DA in aqueous solution are firstly compared. As depicted in Fig. 3 a, after addition of 25 μM DA solution, the photoluminescence intensity of ZGC/HZIF-8 obviously decrease with the quenching efficiency reaching nearly 59%. In sharp contrast, no evident quenching phenomenon is observed in ZGC system under the same experimental operation. It is thus obvious that HZIF-8 plays an important role in accelerating the luminescence quenching of ZGC by DA. The underlying mechanism will be discussed and verified in detail later. Hence, the ZGC/HZIF-8 nanohybrids are demonstrated to be promising probes for DA detection.

Under the optimal assay conditions (presented in detail in Figs. S6–S8), ZGC/HZIF-8 composites were further employed for autoluminescence-free DA biosensing. Since DA is one of the most important excitatory neurotransmitters and abnormal DA concentration can lead to various behavioral disorders, accurate detection of DA in biological fluids is of great importance, especially in serum. However, as we know, the complex serum contains certain biological chromophores, which display strong autofluorescence under excitation, seriously interfering the fluorescence assay. Fortunately, the proposed ZGC/HZIF-8 composites exhibit excellent long-live luminescence after removing the excitation light, providing a possible strategy for background-free DA detection in serum. The ability of ZGC/HZIF-8 composites to suppress background autofluorescence was then tested before DA quantitative detection. To start with, the photoluminescence and persistent luminescence spectra of the serum were primarily measured and presented in Fig. S9. The photoluminescence curve shows that human serum possesses intense autofluorescence in the range of 600–750 nm, but persistent luminescence spectrum in this interval shows that self-fluorescence has been eliminated entirely because of its transience after withdrawing excitation light source. Furthermore, healthy human serum was added to the ZGC/HZIF-8 solutions to test the inhibitory effect of ZGC/HZIF-8 in suppression of autofluorescence. As shown in Fig. 3 b, the strong fluorescence of serum matrix in the section of 600–750 nm inevitably hinders the emission peak of ZGC/HZIF-8 at 700 nm. However, it can be clearly seen that there is an independent and complete emission peak at ~700 nm belonging to ZGC/HZIF-8 after the external excitation light is removed, as evidenced by persistent luminescence spectrum. These results indicate that human serum auto-fluorescence disappears completely because of its transiency without external excitation and the afterglow luminescence of ZGC/HZIF-8 can effectively avoid the interference caused by self-fluorescence in organisms. This phenomenon will pave the way for developing ZGC/HZIF-8-based biological monitoring.

Thereafter, under the optimized condition, the response of ZGC/HZIF-8 to different concentrations of DA was tested and recorded. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the persistent luminescence intensity of ZGC/HZIF-8 gradually decreased with increasing DA concentration. Corresponding color change of ZGC/HZIF-8 reacting with diverse concentrations of DA is displayed in the illustration. With the increase of DA concentration, the color of the white solution becomes darker and ultimately turned into black observable by naked eyes under sunlight. The reason for formation of black solution is probably due to the production of black polymer PDA around ZGC/HZIF-8. In addition, the red fluorescence of ZGC/HZIF-8 solution weakens with the increase of DA concentration, which can be confirmed by the fluorescence diagrams under 254 nm UV irradiation in the illustration.

Moreover, the quenching efficiency (F0-F)/F0 ratio (F0 and F representing the persistent luminescence intensities of ZGC/HZIF-8 in the absence and presence of DA) and the concentration of DA exists linear relationship in the range of 0.0025–25 μM and 25–75 μM, as seen from Fig. 4 b. The coefficients of determination (R2) are 0.9995 and 0.9996 fitted, respectively, and the corresponding limit of detection (LOD) was as low as 0.0010 μM estimated using 3σ criterion. Table S1 shows the comparison of several DA analytical platforms reported in recent years, indicating the proposed method is equipped with superior or comparable performance in terms of detection range and LOD.

Furthermore, the persistent luminescence of ZGC/HZIF-8 in the presence or absence of DA was monitored via visualized decay images (Fig. 4 c). Following the same trend as above spectral results, the persistent luminescence intensity of the samples gradually attenuates with increasing DA concentration, as seen from the horizontal row in the figure. Another conclusion is that the long afterglow lifetime decreases with DA concentration increasing. Taken together, these results reveal that ZGC/HZIF-8 nanoparticles can act as an excellent working platform for dopamine sensing with high sensitivity.

As demonstrated above, ultrahigh sensitivity for DA determination has been realized based on DA-induced luminescence quenching of the elaborately designed ZGC/HZIF-8 nanohybrids. As for the quenching mechanism, it is necessary to confirm the exact quencher, given that both DA and PDA have fluorescence quenching ability as previously reported [44,45]. Since oxygen plays an important role in DA polymerization, we compare the luminescence changes of ZGC/HZIF-8 after incubation with DA under aerobic and nitrogen atmospheres, respectively. It turns out that oxygen is necessary for luminescence quenching (Fig. S10), suggesting that it is the PDA that functions as a quencher in ZGC/HZIF-8 based sensing platform. The production of PDA can be further verified by UV–vis and FT-IR spectra (Fig. S11). The quenching effect presumably results from PET mechanism, wherein the excited photoelectron of ZGC will be instantaneously captured by PDA as favorable electron acceptors [46].

When it comes to the ultrahigh sensitivity of the proposed sensor, in addition to the excitation-free persistent luminescence of ZGC element, such satisfactory sensing performance is greatly dependent on the involvement of HZIF-8, which has been preliminarily proofed via the comparison of luminescence response between ZGC and ZGC/HZIF-8 nanohybrids toward DA (Fig. 3 a). Herein the unique morphological features play a decisive role. On the one hand, the bouquet-like HZIF-8 with rough surface provides large amounts of anchoring sites for ZGC, achieving the enrichment of optical elements within HZIF-8 carrier matrix. On the other hand, thanks to their high surface area, hierarchical micro- and mesoporous structure and outstanding molecular adsorption capabilities, HZIF-8 can serve as a preconcentrator to efficiently accumulate DA into the porous MOF matrices [43]. That means a tremendous amount of DA molecules has been brought in close proximity to ZGC, thus resulting in greatly improved sensitivity.

Moreover, the specific structural feature of HZIF-8 may also play a critical role in improving the sensitivity. When the added DA molecules are preconcentrated around ZGC/HZIF-8, the alkalescent 2-MI in HZIF-8 has the ability to promote the in-situ oxidative self-polymerization of surrounding DA into PDA, and the latter has a superior chelating capability with Zn2+ than does 2-MI, probably leading to the broken up of Zn2+–2-MI coordination bond and even the collapse of HZIF-8 structure [47–49]. To validate this possibility, we subsequently studied the morphology of the products of ZGC, ZIF-8, HZIF-8 and ZGC/HZIF-8 nanomaterials before and after reacting with DA of diverse concentrations (Figs. S12–S15). The results show that the morphology of ZGC shows little change even in high concentration of DA. In sharp contrast, great changes have taken place in the shape of the others, particularly ZGC/HZIF-8. As the concentrations of DA increase, appearance of ZGC/HZIF-8 becomes more irregular, indicating the gradual collapse of HZIF-8. So we can conclude that Zn2+-2-MI coordination bond was broken up, while the released Zn2+ ions were simultaneously chelated by PDA.

To sum up, the sensing performance of the proposed sensor benefits from the unique morphological and structural features of HZIF-8, as well as ultra-small size and background-free persistent luminescence of ZGC PLNPs. ZGC PLNPs with ultra-small size can be more electrostatically adsorbed on the surface of HZIF-8 due to the large specific surface area. DA can be concentrated by HZIF-8 and is more inclined to be oxidized into PDA around ZGC/HZIF-8 rather than in solution because of the partial alkaline environment provided by 2-MI. Therefore the luminescence of ZGC can be effectively quenched by the neighboring PDA via PET mechanism. With the increasing concentration of DA, HZIF-8 structure is broken up due to the synergistic interaction between Zn2+ and PDA, which may lead to different linear correlation at low and higher DA concentrations (Fig. 4 b).

Due to the complexity of human blood, high specificity should also be considered in addition to the sensitivity of the sensing platform. Therefore, several common amino acids in human body and neurotransmitters with similar chemical structure to DA were investigated. As shown in Fig. 5 a, ZGC/HZIF-8 can only be quenched when DA is added. The other sixteen kinds of interfering substances including histidine (His), valine (Val), alanine (Ala), glutamic acid (Glu), proline (Pro), phenylalanine (Phe), glycine (Gly), leucine (Leu), cysteine (Cys), arginine (Arg), glutathione (GSH), ascorbic acid (AA), glucose (G), uric acid (UA), Levodopa (L-Dopa) and epinephrine (E) have little effect on quenching efficiency even if their concentrations are five times that of DA. In particular, 10 μM of DA added to the above interfering substances result in 47% of the original persistent luminescence intensity ( Fig. 5 b). It turns out that ZGC/HZIF-8 provides high selectivity for DA.

Last but not least, the repeatability and stability of this working platform are also investigated. We repeatedly excited ZGC/HZIF-8 and recorded the fluorescence intensity, finding that the relative standard deviation (RSD) is less than 3%. Furthermore, ZGC/HZIF-8 shows long time storage stability. Fig. S16 shows that the intensities of ZGC/HZIF-8 in the absence or presence of DA remain unchanged for a long time, suggesting the platform is robust in sensing applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative DA determination in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: These results prove that ZGC/HZIF-8 based detection platform possesses excellent stability, sensitivity and selectivity, and is promising for practical application. To demonstrate this, the sensor is applied to serum detection from healthy people. Herein, DA solution is added to serum matrix diluted 100 times with Tris-HCl buffer (2.5 mM, pH 7.4) by a standard addition experiment. As shown in Table S2 , the recoveries of DA is in the range of 98.2–104.7%, and RSD is between 0.4% and 2.2%, which are acceptable for quantitative detection performed in biological samples.

In summary, a platform fabricated by integrating persistent luminescence ZGC PLNPs with HZIF-8 has been first employed in DA sensing. In this system, DA is selectively oxidized into PDA around HZIF-8 due to its preconcentration effect and alkaline environment provided by 2-MI. Meanwhile, photo-excited electrons are transferred from ZGC to oxidized PDA, contributing to luminescence quenching of ZGC/HZIF-8 composites. This platform exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity towards DA detection. Furthermore, it has been experimentally proved that ZGC/HZIF-8 can be applied to quantitative detection of DA in serum samples from healthy people with the recoveries in the range of 98.2–104.7%. The proposed ZGC/HZIF-8 nanohybrids can be a promising candidate for disease diagnosis related to DA.

Tianlin Yao: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Gaoqiu Dong: Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Data curation. Shiyu Qian: Investigation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. Yu Cui and Xiang Chen: Investigation. Tingting Tan: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Lingling Li: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22074066).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.131470.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Tianlin Yao received her bachelor degree from Nanjing Medical University. Currently, she is a graduate student in school of pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University. Her research focuses on optical biosensors.

Gaoqiu Dong received her bachelor degree from Nanjing Medical University. Currently, she is a graduate student in school of pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University. Her research focuses on preparation of functional nanomaterials.

Shiyu Qian received her bachelor degree from Nanjing Medical University. Currently, she is a graduate student in school of pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University. Her research focuses on long-lasting luminescent nanomaterials.
Yu Cui received her bachelor degree from Changzhi Medical College. Currently, she is a graduate student in school of pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University. Her research focuses on optical probes.
Xiang Chen received her bachelor degree from Qingdao University. She is now a graduate student in school of pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University. Her works focuses on nano-drug carriers.
Tingting Tan received her master degree from Nanjing University Medical School. Currently, she is a doctor in the Department of Laboratory Medicine, Nanjing Drum Tower Hospital.

Lingling Li received her Ph.D. degree from Nanjing University. Currently, she is a professor in school of pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University. Her major research interests mainly focus on nanomaterials-based biosensors and drug delivery systems.
